title: About Dr. Aviv Regev
text:
Imagine a world
where as patients are treated we have
this
road map for disease: We know which genes
are important,
we know which cells are important, we
know what the genes are doing inside the
cells,
we look at the patient's own tissues and
samples, and based on that, we have the
pharmacopoeia
and the diagnostics in order to decide
on the care and provide it.
I was immediately captivated by cells and 
their inner workings and all of their
intricacies,
but i also felt this palpable gap
because unlike genetics and evolution
where we had these clean and theoretical
and mathematical descriptions with which
to reason about biology
in cell and molecular biology, we didn't.
And I felt that that was really ripe
for looking at things in a different way,
and it remained my problem
ever since.  So our bodies are made of
37.2
trillion cells and at first glance you
might assume that they're all the same
because they all have the same set of
instructions
in their DNA. But of course the cells are
not the same:
They look different and they do
different things.
Imagine that your cells are like
different pieces of fruit.
What we used to be able to do in the lab
is take all of these different pieces of
fruit
and essentially put them through the lab
equivalent of a fruit
blender and get the lab equivalent of a
fruit
smoothie. What we can do today in single-
cell genomics can be thought of as the
era
of the fruit salad with many different
kinds of fruits and each of them
analyzed separately and individually and
very. very rapidly.
That's single-cell genomics.
Now you may ask yourself, why does that
matter?
Well, one of the reasons that it matters
is because of human disease.
If we want to understand the roles of
genes in disease,
we need to know where these genes are
used and for this we need to know which
cells we have.
The Human Cell Atlas you can think of as
a map that is trying to describe the
human body
in a coherent and consistent way. Just
like in Google Maps, where you can zoom
out and you see everything and you can
zoom in
and you can look at extremely fine
detail, so would be the Human Cell
Atlas.
You would understand where things are,
you would understand their internal
properties, but you would also understand
their relations.
It's something that felt like a deep
mission and an inner calling and that
no one person, one lab, one institute or
one country should do it alone,
but we should really do it together,
because
humans are diverse, their cells are
diverse and scientists are diverse.
Receiving this prize I am grateful for
all of those people with whom I work:
the wonderful open scientific community
that i belong to across the globe.
I'm also filled with sense of commitment:
not just
gratitude for what I have done but
commitment for what I'm going to do in
the future: for ways in which I can do more myself
and for ways in which I can
empower others to also pursue ambitious
visions.
